full_response:
Step-1:  Levi–Civita symbol in 3-space  
The Euclidean Levi-Civita symbol ε_{ijk} is defined by

• ε_{ijk}=+1  if (i,j,k) is an even permutation of (1,2,3)  
• ε_{ijk}=−1  if (i,j,k) is an odd permutation of (1,2,3)  
• ε_{ijk}= 0   if any two indices are equal.

It is totally antisymmetric: ε_{ijk}=−ε_{jik}=−ε_{ikj}=…

Step-2:  Cross product in ε-notation  
Let a⃗ and b⃗ be vectors with components a_i , b_i  (i=1,2,3).  
Define a vector c⃗ with components

c_i ≡ ε_{ijk} a_j b_k .        (summation over repeated indices)

Explicitly,

c_1 = ε_{1jk} a_j b_k = ε_{123} a_2 b_3 + ε_{132} a_3 b_2 = a_2 b_3 − a_3 b_2  
c_2 = ε_{2jk} a_j b_k = ε_{213} a_1 b_3 + ε_{231} a_3 b_1 = a_3 b_1 − a_1 b_3  
c_3 = ε_{3jk} a_j b_k = ε_{312} a_1 b_2 + ε_{321} a_2 b_1 = a_1 b_2 − a_2 b_1

These are precisely the Cartesian components of a⃗ × b⃗ .  Hence

( a⃗ × b⃗ )_i = ε_{ijk} a_j b_k .            (1)

Step-3:  Curl of a vector field  
For a vector field f⃗ (x⃗ ) with components f_k(x), the curl is defined by

(∇ × f⃗ )_i = ε_{ijk} ∂_j f_k ,             (2)

where ∂_j ≡ ∂/∂x_j.  Writing out the components verifies that Eq. (2) reproduces the usual textbook formulas:

(∇ × f⃗ )_x = ∂_y f_z − ∂_z f_y , etc.

Both (1) and (2) follow immediately from the total antisymmetry of ε_{ijk}.

\boxed{(a\times b)_i=\varepsilon_{ijk}\,a_j b_k,\qquad (\nabla\times\mathbf f)_i=\varepsilon_{ijk}\,\partial_j f_k} 

Confidence: 96%
